ground_truth.title: Robots in Daily Life: Not Just Sci-Fi Anymore
ground_truth.author: Adnan Arif
ground_truth.main_content: Once confined to the realm of science fiction, robots have transitioned from fantastical depictions in literature and film to tangible entities that are increasingly integral to our daily lives. This transition is not merely a technological curiosity but a significant evolution that presents both opportunities and challenges. The technical problem we face today is not about creating robots that can perform tasks; it's about integrating these robots seamlessly into human environments to enhance productivity, safety, and quality of life. As robots become more prevalent, understanding their underlying technologies and real-world applications is crucial. Robots today are employed in various domains such as healthcare, agriculture, logistics, and domestic environments. Their deployment raises questions about technical implementation, human-robot interaction, and socio-economic impacts. This article delves into the technical intricacies of robots in daily life, exploring their architecture, functionalities, and the measurable outcomes they deliver. Key concepts include Robot: An autonomous or semi-autonomous machine capable of performing tasks by processing data and executing actions based on pre-programmed instructions or adaptive learning algorithms. Artificial Intelligence (AI): The simulation of human intelligence in machines, enabling them to learn, reason, and self-correct. AI is a cornerstone of modern robotics, particularly in decision-making and adaptation. Machine Learning (ML): A subset of AI that involves the use of algorithms and statistical models to enable machines to improve their performance on tasks through experience. Sensors: Devices that detect changes in the environment and provide data inputs to robots. Common sensors include cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic sensors, and tactile sensors. Actuators: Components responsible for moving or controlling a mechanism or system, typically converting electrical signals into physical motion. Human-Robot Interaction (HRI): The study of interactions between humans and robots, focusing on improving communication, collaboration, and safety. The architecture of a robot typically consists of several integrated subsystems: Perception System, Processing Unit, Control System, Communication Interface, and Power Supply. A notable example is the da Vinci Surgical System, a teleoperated robotic surgical system employed in over six million procedures globally. Its architecture includes a 3D high-definition vision system, wristed instruments that bend and rotate far greater than the human hand, and a console that provides the surgeon with intuitive control. Studies indicate a significant reduction in hospital stays by an average of 1.2 days compared to traditional surgery, and complication rates are also typically lower.

Robots today are employed in various domains such as healthcare, agriculture, logistics, and domestic environments.
Robot: An autonomous or semi-autonomous machine capable of performing tasks by processing data and executing actions based on pre-programmed instructions or adaptive learning algorithms.
The architecture of a robot typically consists of several integrated subsystems: Perception System, Processing Unit, Control System, Communication Interface, and Power Supply.
The da Vinci system, a teleoperated robotic surgical system, has been employed in over six million procedures globally.
